summary: Officially documented NTFS control for last-access timestamp updates. Good for reducing disk I/O and latency on large file trees, but some backup and archival tools rely on last-access data and can behave differently when it is disabled.

setting.name: NTFS Last Access Time Updates
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can update a file or directory's last-access timestamp when it is accessed. Disabling these updates can reduce some disk overhead, but software that depends on last-access data can lose that signal.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable last-access updates on performance-focused systems, servers, or large file trees where they want less metadata churn and lower disk I/O.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off can improve file-system efficiency, but software that depends on last-access timestamps can stop working as expected.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents the fsutil behavior disablelastaccess parameter as the control for HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate. The current fsutil documentation defines 1 as disabling the updates and 0 as enabling them. Microsoft performance-tuning guidance also states that NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate is set by default to 1 on clean modern Windows installations, and the AgeStore documentation says last-access data is disabled by default on Windows Vista and later unless explicitly enabled.

allowed_values.label: Last-access updates enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Windows records last-access timestamp updates on NTFS volumes.

allowed_values.label: Last-access updates disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not update last-access timestamps on NTFS volumes.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate = 1, which matches the documented disabled state.

windows_defaults.label: Modern Windows default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Clean modern Windows installations using the default NTFS baseline

states.rationale: Modern Windows uses the disabled state by default for this setting.

windows_defaults.notes: Microsoft notes that clean installations already use NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate = 1 by default, while earlier or upgraded systems may differ.

recommended_profiles.label: Modern Windows default
intended_for: Large file trees and server-style workloads
avoid_for: Workloads that explicitly depend on last-access timestamps

states.rationale: Keep the modern Windows baseline that disables last-access updates.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the lower-I/O modern Windows baseline for NTFS metadata.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You run backup, archival, or maintenance tooling that needs last-access timestamps.
tradeoffs: Disk I/O and metadata churn are reduced.
Software that depends on last-access data can stop working as expected.

recommended_profiles.label: Last-access tracking enabled
avoid_for: Large file trees where metadata churn matters
recommended_profiles.good_when: You need last-access timestamps for maintenance or compatibility reasons.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the lower-overhead modern Windows baseline.
tradeoffs: Programs that rely on last-access data keep working with that signal.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: fsutil behavior
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents disablelastaccess as the control that updates HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate, where 1 disables updates and 0 enables them.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Performance Tuning Web Servers
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem and says clean modern Windows installations set it to 1 by default.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Using AgeStore
evidence.summary: Microsoft notes that last-access data is disabled by default on Windows Vista and later, and shows fsutil behavior set disablelastaccess 0 to enable and 1 to disable the data collection.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact registry path, values, and default direction for modern Windows, and the app writes the documented disabled state.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: The system-global switch NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate (REG_DWORD) 1 is located under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem and is set by default to 1.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft's web-server performance guidance provides the exact registry path and modern default of 1. The matching fsutil behavior documentation states that disablelastaccess {1|0} disables (1) or enables (0) updates to the Last Access Time stamp.
